winner: In Wall Street, they've just announced a new policy: From now on, all trades must be executed in pigeon-peck Morse code. Seems like Twitter is no longer required for trading, only for tweeting.

candidates.text: Why did the stockbroker refuse to trade near a bird feeder? Because he didn't want to squawk-erage his portfolio!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 8.0, "reason": "Creative pun on 'squawk' and 'average,' with a clear connection to the headline's wordplay."}

candidates.text: Why don't financial tweeters ever barter tweets for stocks? Because they fear a Twitter-action!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke has some wordplay with 'Twitter-action,' but it's a bit forced and not clearly connected to the headline. It lacks specificity and surprise, making it somewhat generic."}

candidates.text: Why did WallStreetBear refuse to follow Twitter trends? Because he didn't want to trade pawdcasts for stock market claws!
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke has some wordplay with 'pawdcasts' and 'stock market claws,' but the connection to the headline is weak and the humor feels forced."}

candidates.text: A man walked into a Twitter shop to buy some trades, but the cashier said, "Sir, this isn't a trading marketplace. We only sell tweets here!" The man replied, "Well, I was hoping for a 'tweet-for-trade' deal." The cashier looked confused and asked, "What on earth is a tweet-for-trade?" The man replied, "Don't trade where you tweet, after all.
candidates.judge_raw: {"score": 6.5, "reason": "The joke has a clever twist with the 'tweet-for-trade' pun and ties back to the headline, but it's somewhat generic and lacks the sharp surprise and specificity that would make it stand out in a humor competition."}
